Buyer's Journey Guide

Purchaser Acknowledgement Form

Please complete your details and sign before submitting an offer to purchase a property.

H HOMETOWN
@realty
Licensed REAA 2008
Hereby Acknowledge the Following
  1. I/We were advised of the Hometown Real Estate Limited in-house complaints process. Refer atrealty.co.nz
  2. I/We were advised that we must provide proof to our solicitor of an IRD tax number in my/our name; and a New Zealand bank account number in my/our name.
  3. I/We confirm that we were advised to seek legal advice before entering into any agreement or contract.
  4. I/We confirm that at the time we entered into this agreement we did so freely without influence or duress.
  5. I/We have been made aware that any house built of monolithic cladding (Polystyrene or Harditex with a plastered finish, typically 1990–2006) may be prone to weather tightness issues and we have been advised to seek independent expert advice.
  6. I/We have been made aware that any house built in the 1970s–1980s may have Dux Quest plumbing systems. We recommend seeking advice from a qualified specialist if concerned.
  7. Asbestos was a common building material that may be present in houses. We recommend seeking advice from a qualified specialist if concerned.
Disclosures

I/We acknowledge that we were advised that the provisions:

I/We acknowledge that the following matters (if any) were specifically disclosed to us during the transaction or prior to signing the Contract.

Overseas Investment Act
I/we understand that residential property purchases are now subject to the provisions of the Overseas Investment Act 2005 (OIA). Before any residential property is transferred to me/us, my/our lawyer will require me/us to complete a Residential Land Statement certifying that I/we meet the eligibility criteria.

If I/we require OIA consent and do not obtain it or our application is declined, we will not be able to settle and may incur liability to the vendor.

If uncertain about eligibility, you must make your offer subject to obtaining Overseas Investment Office consent.
Auctions
You must not bid on a property at auction unless you are able to buy the property on an unconditional basis.
You may incur liability to the vendor if you purchase at auction where you do not meet the OIA eligibility criteria.
Customer Due Diligence
I/we understand and acknowledge that before my lawyer can act for me/us, they must complete customer due diligence (CDD) on me/us under the Anti-Money Laundering and Countering Financing of Terrorism Act 2009 (AML/CFTA).

If my/our lawyer cannot complete CDD and cannot act for me/us as a result, I/we may not be able to satisfy conditions under the agreement or settle the property purchase.
Signatures

If purchasing as trustees of a trust, all trustees must sign. If a company is purchasing, signing confirms you are duly authorised to sign on the company's behalf.

Purchaser 1

Purchaser 2 (if applicable)

Purchaser 4 (if applicable)

Your Real Estate Buying Journey

Understanding the end-to-end real estate process in New Zealand

@realty
settled.govt.nz
Buying a home is one of the biggest financial decisions you'll ever make. This guide walks you through each stage. Visit settled.govt.nz for full tools, checklists and videos. Brought to you by the Real Estate Authority (REA).
Stage 1 — Thinking of buying?
Agree on your goalsDecide what you're looking for in a home and what your goals are. Talk to friends and family.
Sort your financeHow much can you borrow or afford? Investigate and understand your loan options before you take out a home loan.
Build your support crewYou'll need a lawyer, an insurer, and a bank or mortgage broker. You may need a property inspector later.
Learn the lingoLearn about methods of sale, types of property ownership and how to identify risks and potential issues.
Stage 2 — Finding a property
Attend open homes. By now you'll know what you're looking for and what red flags to watch out for. If you like a home, start researching it in more detail. It may take a while to find the ideal property.
Stage 3 — Researching the property
Do your homeworkA property inspection report and LIM are helpful. The more you know before making an offer, the better.
Ask for a title searchA record of title is essential — it tells you if there are any restrictions against the property.
Understand issues & hazardsLearn about any significant repairs needed and any risks with the property.
⚠ Important
Don't make an offer on a property before seeking legal advice.
Stage 4 — Making an offer
Advertised price / NegotiationSeller sets a price. You can offer more or less. No specific deadline.
Deadline saleSeller sets a date. You can make an offer at any time before that date.
TenderConfidential written offers before a specified end date. Seller doesn't have to accept the highest offer.
AuctionFast-paced public sale. If you win, you are committed to purchase immediately — no conditions. Have your finance and due diligence sorted BEFORE you bid. You must pay the deposit on auction day.
Multi-offer processIf another buyer also makes an offer, it becomes a multi-offer process. Put your best foot forward — you may not get a second chance. The seller can choose the offer that works best for them, not necessarily the highest price. Agents must explain the process clearly to all buyers.
Stage 5 — Settling and moving in
Confirm your financeConfirm with your lender. Banks will ask for more info about the property and proof of insurance before settlement.
Meet conditionsAny contract conditions must be met by your specified dates. Talk to your lawyer if you need an extension.
Plan for settlement dayPre-settlement inspection, finance and insurance must all be in place. Many things happen in sequence.
Settle on settlement dayYour lawyer, the seller's lawyer and the banks all work together. Plan to move in the day after settlement day.
Getting help If you have a problem with a real estate agent you can't resolve directly, contact the Real Estate Authority (REA).
Call: 0800 367 7322 · Email: [email protected] · Web: rea.govt.nz
A sale and purchase agreement is a legally binding contract. Always get legal advice before making your offer.
Guide brought to you by the Real Estate Authority via settled.govt.nz
